data.title: PREIMAGE
data.comment: Build the BIP341 sighash message that all three signers will sign.

data.title: ROUND 1: NONCES
data.comment: Each signer generates a nonce pair. Public nonces are aggregated into one shared nonce.

data.title: TRANSACTION
data.comment: Place the signature into a Taproot key-path witness and verify the spend.

data.title: ROUND 2: SIGN
data.comment: Each signer computes a partial signature. The coordinator combines all three into one 64-byte Schnorr signature.

data.title: KEYAGG
data.comment: Aggregate three pubkeys into one key, apply Taproot tweak, derive P2TR address.

data.title: SIGNERS
data.comment: Three signers each generate a secret key and compressed public key.

fields.comment: 32-byte transaction ID in reversed byte order
fields.label: TXID[32]:

fields.comment: 4-byte sequence (LE). Use fdffffff for RBF, ffffffff for final
fields.label: SEQUENCE[4]:

fields.comment: Amount in satoshis as 8-byte little-endian hex
fields.label: AMOUNT[8]:

fields.comment: Script length as varint (usually 16 for P2WPKH, 19 for P2PKH)
fields.label: SCRIPT_LENGTH:

fields.comment: Native SegWit: empty | Nested: 0014[hash] | Legacy: full script
fields.label: SCRIPT_SIG:

fields.comment: ⚠️ EVERY input needs witness! Legacy='00', SegWit=actual data
fields.label: WITNESS_DATA:

ungrouped.comment: Transaction version
ungrouped.label: VERSION[4]:

ungrouped.comment: Include ONLY if ANY input is SegWit (00), empty for legacy-only
ungrouped.label: MARKER[1]:

ungrouped.comment: Include ONLY if ANY input is SegWit (01), empty for legacy-only
ungrouped.label: FLAG[1]:

ungrouped.comment: Number of inputs (varint)
ungrouped.label: INPUT_COUNT:
ITEM_COUNT (1B):
ungrouped.comment: Usually 0x01 for key-path

ungrouped.label: SIG_LENGTH (VarInt):
ungrouped.comment: Length of Schnorr sig (0x40 for 64 bytes)

ungrouped.label: EPOCH (1B):
ungrouped.comment: Version byte for future sighash upgrades (always 00 now)

ungrouped.label: SIGHASH_TYPE (1B):
ungrouped.comment: Taproot sighash flag (BIP342 semantics)

ungrouped.label: VERSION (4B LE):
ungrouped.comment: Transaction version (little-endian)

ungrouped.label: LOCKTIME (4B LE):
ungrouped.comment: nLockTime (little-endian)

ungrouped.label: HASH_PREVOUTS (32B):
ungrouped.comment: Shared across inputs: sha256 of all outpoints

ungrouped.label: HASH_AMOUNTS (32B):
ungrouped.comment: NEW in Taproot: sha256 of all input amounts

ungrouped.label: HASH_SCRIPTS (32B):
ungrouped.comment: NEW in Taproot: sha256 of all input scriptPubKeys

ungrouped.label: HASH_SEQUENCES (32B):
ungrouped.comment: Shared across inputs: sha256 of all sequences

ungrouped.label: HASH_OUTPUTS (32B, optional):
ungrouped.placeholder: <sha256 of outputs, leave empty for NONE>
ungrouped.comment: Sha256 of all outputs; toggle "Allow Empty/None" for SIGHASH_NONE/SINGLE.

ungrouped.label: SPEND_TYPE (1B):
ungrouped.comment: 00 = key-path, 02 = script-path (annex sets bit 0x01)

ungrouped.label: INPUT_INDEX (4B LE):
ungrouped.comment: Little-endian vin index being signed (0-based; 00000000 for single-input txs)

ungrouped.label: VERSION[4]:
ungrouped.comment: Transaction version

ungrouped.label: MARKER[1]:
ungrouped.comment: Include ONLY if ANY input is SegWit (00), empty for legacy-only

ungrouped.label: FLAG[1]:
ungrouped.comment: Include ONLY if ANY input is SegWit (01), empty for legacy-only

ungrouped.label: INPUT_COUNT:
ungrouped.comment: Number of inputs (varint)

fields.label: SCRIPT_SIG:
fields.comment: Native SegWit: empty | Nested: 0014[hash] | Legacy: full script

fields.label: WITNESS_DATA:

data.content:
# Lesson 14: MuSig2 Key-Path Multisig (BIP327)

## N-of-N: When Everyone Must Agree

Some Bitcoin setups require all parties to sign every spend:
joint custody between partners, corporate treasury, or
Lightning channels where both sides agree on every state update.

For these n-of-n cases, Taproot offers something powerful:
aggregate all keys into one output key with MuSig2, spend via
key-path. One signature, 64 bytes, nothing revealed on-chain.
Indistinguishable from a single-signer spend — compared to
~271 bytes and full policy disclosure with CHECKSIGADD (L13).

For fallback — someone unavailable — define script leaves with
smaller quorums, timelocks, or recovery keys (see previous flow examples).
None visible unless used.

## Why It Works

Schnorr signatures have a property called linearity: partial
signatures from multiple signers can be summed into one valid
signature for the aggregate key — without anyone ever knowing
that key's private key. Each signer uses only their own secret.

ECDSA doesn't have this property — which is why MuSig2
requires Schnorr (BIP340) and wasn't possible before Taproot.

## Drawbacks

All signers must be online together for two interactive rounds.
If anyone drops out mid-session, it restarts. CHECKSIGADD
signers act independently — no coordination needed.

Each signing session requires fresh random nonces — generated,
used once, destroyed. Nonce reuse across different transactions can expose the signer's private key (see Exercise 2 below).

## What This Flow Builds

A complete 3-of-3 MuSig2 key-path spend in six stages:

    SIGNERS → KEYAGG → PREIMAGE → ROUND 1 → ROUND 2 → TRANSACTION
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
# The Flow — Step by Step

## SIGNERS

Three participants — Alice, Bob, Charlie — each generate
their own private key and public key. Same key generation
as every prior lesson. MuSig2 doesn't require special keys.

No one ever sees anyone else's private key — not during
key aggregation, not during signing, not ever.

## KEYAGG

Combine three public keys into a single aggregate key, then
derive a standard Taproot address from it. On-chain, the
result looks exactly like a single-signer address.

Why not just add the three keys together? Because a
malicious signer could craft a fake key that cancels out
the others — taking sole control of the "shared" funds.
BIP327 derives each signer's coefficient from the ordered
full key list, then computes the aggregate key as a weighted
sum. That binds every contribution to the entire participant
set and prevents rogue-key cancellation.

Same keys, different order → different coefficients →
different aggregate key. All signers must agree on one
exact ordering before they aggregate.

After aggregation, the aggregate key P gets the same
Taproot tweak as Lesson 11 to produce the output key Q
and the P2TR address. Fund this address on testnet.

TapTweak hashes build Q; TapSighash hashes (in PREIMAGE)
build msg. Same SHA-256, different tags, different jobs.

What leaves this group:
- Output key Q → used in both signing rounds
- Tweak scalar t → needed by ROUND 2 for signature correction
- P2TR address → fund this on testnet

## PREIMAGE

Before anyone can sign, all parties need to agree on
exactly what they're signing. This group builds the
sighash message — a 32-byte hash that commits to every
detail of the transaction: prevout TXID, amounts, outputs,
scriptPubKeys, sequence, locktime, version.

Change any single field and msg changes — which means all
existing nonces and partial signatures become useless and
must be redone.

This is the same BIP341 sighash construction from
Lesson 11. MuSig2 can sign any message at the protocol
level, but in a Taproot key-path spend the message must
be the BIP341 TapSighash digest that verifiers will
recompute.

The group also constructs the destination output — where
the funds go after spending.

## ROUND 1: NONCES

This is the first of two interactive rounds — and the first
time a nonce appears explicitly on the canvas.

What is a nonce? Every digital signature — ECDSA and
Schnorr alike — uses a secret random value called a nonce
during signing. It masks the private key inside the
signature equation. Without it, anyone could extract the
private key from a single signature using basic algebra.

In every previous lesson, you never saw the nonce because
it was generated and used entirely inside the signing
function (Schnorr Sign, ECDSA Sign). Even in Lesson 13
with three signers, each produced their own independent
signature — each with its own hidden nonce. No coordination
needed.

MuSig2 is different. All three signers produce one shared
signature, which means they need one shared nonce. No
single signer can produce it alone — all three must
contribute a piece, then combine those pieces before
anyone can sign. That's why the nonce appears on the
canvas for the first time, and why this exchange step
exists.

On the canvas, each signer feeds one fresh random seed
into NonceGen (along with their secret key, the output
key Q, and msg). NonceGen internally derives two secret
nonce values from that single seed — this is standard
BIP327, not specific to our implementation. Two nonce
components let MuSig2 stay secure even when multiple
signing sessions run at the same time.

NonceGen outputs:
- secnonce — secret, kept locally
- pubnonce — public, shared with others

A coordinator collects all three pubnonces and combines
them into one aggregate nonce (aggnonce) via NonceAgg.

⚠️ The random seed must be fresh every session. Reusing a secnonce across different transactions can expose the signer's private key — see Exercise 2.

secnonce must be stored between rounds — it is needed
in ROUND 2 for partial signing — then erased immediately
after use.

## ROUND 2: SIGN

Each signer now has everything they need: the message,
the aggregate nonce, and the key aggregation context.
They each independently compute a partial signature
using their own secret key and secret nonce.

Each signer must independently validate the transaction
details that produced msg before signing — otherwise a
malicious coordinator could trick them into signing a
different spend.

The coordinator collects the three partial signatures
and combines them into one standard 64-byte Schnorr
signature. The Taproot tweak is accounted for during
this aggregation — the final signature is valid under
the output key Q, not the raw aggregate key P.

The coordinator only handles public data. A malicious
coordinator can stall or abort a session, but cannot
forge a valid signature — even colluding with all but
one signer.

The result is indistinguishable from a single-signer
BIP340 signature. No verifier can tell that three
parties were involved.

## TRANSACTION

The signature from ROUND 2 is placed into a Taproot
key-path witness — just the signature, nothing else.
No script, no control block, no public keys revealed.

In this lesson the signature is 64 bytes because
SIGHASH_DEFAULT is implied. If a non-default sighash
byte were appended, it would become 65 bytes.

The transaction is serialized in two forms: one with
the witness (for broadcast and WTXID), one without
(for the TXID, which must be malleability-free).

Verify Script confirms everything is consistent: the
signature is valid under Q, the scriptPubKey matches,
Taproot consensus rules are satisfied.

TRUE means the entire flow worked — three independent
signers cooperated across two rounds to produce a
spend that looks exactly like a single person paying.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
## Round 1: Nonce Generation & Exchange

Each signer generates two secret nonce scalars (k1, k2) and
publishes the corresponding public nonce points (R1, R2).
These are aggregated into a single aggregate nonce before
anyone signs.

This is the critical safety boundary of MuSig2. The nonce
scalars are derived from the signer's secret key XORed with
auxiliary randomness, then bound to the signer's pubkey, the
aggregate output key Q, and the message. This binding is
defense-in-depth: even if the randomness source repeats, the
nonce derivation still differs as long as any context input
changes.

## Why Randomness Is an Explicit Node

BIP327 NonceGen requires fresh 32-byte auxiliary randomness
for every signing session. In rawBit, we surface this as a
visible Random 32 Bytes node rather than generating it silently
inside the function. This makes the flow fully deterministic
and reproducible — anyone who loads this JSON gets the same
nonces, the same partial signatures, and the same final
transaction. It also makes the dependency visible: you can see
that nonce generation requires randomness, not just keys and a
message.

## The Danger of Nonce Reuse

If the same secnonce is used to sign different messages, an
attacker can extract the signer's private key from the
partial signatures. This is not a theoretical concern — it
is a straightforward algebraic attack (see Exercise 2).
In production MuSig2 implementations, secnonces are generated
once, used once, and erased. In this teaching flow, the nonces
are frozen demo values. Never copy them into a real signing
session.

## What Happens Next

After this round, all three public nonces are aggregated into
one 66-byte aggregate nonce, which every signer uses in Round 2
to compute their partial signature.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
# BIP327 Compliance Notes

This flow implements a BIP327-compliant MuSig2 Taproot
key-path spend. The core signing math — KeyAgg, NonceGen,
NonceAgg, Sign (with internal self-check), and PartialSigAgg
— follows the spec algorithms, uses the correct tagged hashes,
and passes the official BIP327 test vectors.

The following are deliberate scope choices, all explicitly
permitted by BIP327.

## Single Taproot Tweak

This flow applies one x-only tweak (TapTweak) to the aggregate
key. BIP327 defines a general interface that accepts an ordered
list of tweaks, each flagged as x-only or plain, applied in
sequence. This supports advanced protocols that layer multiple
key derivation steps before the final Taproot commitment.

rawBit's signing nodes accept a single optional Taproot tweak.
The underlying accumulator math (gacc, tacc) is the full BIP327
formula and would generalize, but the canvas interface is scoped
to the common Taproot key-path case.

## No Visible Session Context

BIP327 specifies a session_ctx tuple that bundles the aggregate
nonce, pubkey list, tweak list, and message. The spec itself
notes that this is for clarity only: real implementations may
cache, reshape, or eliminate it entirely. rawBit's Sign and
SigAgg nodes recompute session values internally from their
inputs — the math is identical, only the packaging differs.

## PartialSigVerify Is Available as Exercise Node

BIP327 defines PartialSigVerify so honest signers can identify
who sent a bad partial signature and abort with blame. The spec
treats this as an optional application-level feature for
identifiable aborts, not a required step in the signing path.

rawBit includes PartialSigVerify as a node in the sidebar, but
it is intentionally not placed on the main path. Students add
and wire it in Exercise 1.

rawBit's Sign function still includes the spec-mandated internal
self-check (PartialSigVerifyInternal) — each signer verifies
their own partial signature before returning it.

## Fixed Key Order, No KeySort

BIP327 key aggregation is order-dependent: the same set of
pubkeys in a different order produces a different aggregate key.
The spec provides an optional KeySort algorithm for applications
that want a canonical order.

This flow uses a fixed Alice, Bob, Charlie order agreed upon
for the lesson. That is the default BIP327 behavior — sorting
is opt-in, not required.

## 32-Byte Messages Only

BIP327 allows arbitrary-length messages. This flow always signs
a 32-byte BIP341 TapSighash, which is the correct application
message for a Taproot key-path spend. The backend functions do
support variable-length messages for BIP327 vector testing.

## Frozen Demo Nonces

BIP327 requires fresh auxiliary randomness for every signing
session and mandates that secnonces are used once and erased.
This flow surfaces randomness as explicit nodes so the flow is
deterministic and reproducible. All keys and nonces here are
throwaway testnet demo data — never reuse them.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
## Exercises

### Exercise 1: Add PartialSigVerify Nodes

Add three PartialSigVerify nodes to the ROUND 2 group — one
per signer. Wire each node with:
- The partial signature (from the corresponding Sign node)
- The signer's pubnonce (from their NonceGen, default output)
- The signer's compressed pubkey
- The aggnonce (from NonceAgg)
- The TapSighash message
- The TapTweak hash
- All three compressed pubkeys (same order as KeyAgg)

Wiring pattern is intentionally similar to MuSig2 Partial
Sign: aggnonce/message/tweak and PUBKEYS[] use the same
handle numbering. Copy that shape, then swap in the
signer-specific partial signature, pubnonce, and pubkey.

All three should return true. Now try changing one partial
signature by a single hex digit and re-verify. The modified
signer's PartialSigVerify returns false — identifying exactly
who sent a bad partial. That is how MuSig2 enables blame
without revealing anyone's secret key.

### Exercise 2: Nonce Reuse Key Extraction (Advanced)

Duplicate the ROUND 2 group three times. Keep the same
secnonces but change the message each time — for example,
modify the output amount so the TapSighash changes.

In single-signer Schnorr, two signatures with the same
nonce directly reveal the private key. MuSig2 is harder:
the partial signature equation is

    s = k1 + b * k2 + e * a * d    (mod n)

where b depends on the message. Two sessions with the same
secnonce give one equation with two unknowns (k2 and d) —
not enough. But three sessions give two independent
equations, solvable with basic linear algebra.

Hint: subtract pairs of signatures to eliminate k1, then
solve the 2x2 system for k2 and d. Remember that d here
is the effective signing scalar g * gacc * d', where g and
gacc are publicly computable.

This is why BIP327 demands fresh randomness every session
and why secnonces must be erased immediately after signing.
### Exercise 3: Script-Path Fallback 

Build a new flow that uses MuSig2 as the Taproot internal
key but adds a script-path fallback: a 2-of-3 CHECKSIGADD
leaf (from Lesson 13) as a single taptree leaf.

Create two spending paths:
- Key-path: all three signers cooperate via MuSig2.
  One 64-byte signature, no script revealed.
- Script-path: only two of three signers available.
  Reveal the CHECKSIGADD script and control block.

Compare the witness sizes side by side. The key-path spend
is ~66 bytes. The script-path spend is ~200+ bytes and
reveals the fallback policy on-chain. This is the real-world
architecture: MuSig2 for the common case, script leaves
for recovery.
data.title: Text Info Node
